S Skillmaker Studio
Docs GitHub

Skillmaker Studio

Agents do unexpected things. That’s the point. It’s also YOUR problem.

Your agent will do something weird this week. No crash, no error — just off. And it’s on you.

Skillmaker Studio is where commercial-grade skills live — designed, versioned, measured in real agent sessions, and repaired when they drift. On the repo you already have.

View on GitHub See how it works →
install 
$ curl -fsSL https://skillmaker.studio/install.sh | sh

Pre-alpha · v0.2.1 · macOS arm64 + Linux x64

Skills are load-bearing now. Nothing is holding them up.

Your agent skills review code, write commits, summarize incidents — real work rides on them. But a SKILL.md is a paste of unknown pedigree: nobody remembers why it's shaped the way it is, nothing tells you when a model update quietly breaks it, and “it seemed to work” is the entire test suite. The teams who feel this hand-roll their own eval CI. Everyone else finds out in production.

Skillmaker exists so a skill can carry its own evidence — who designed it and why, what it was tested against, how it scored — pinned to the exact version and model that earned the numbers.

When you'd reach for this

Skillmaker isn't for admiring the skill you just wrote. It's for the three moments when “seems to work” stops being an answer.

A new model just shipped.

Your skills were tuned on the last one. Some still work — you can't say which, and rerunning a few prompts by hand doesn't scale past three skills. Adopt the repo in place, add fixtures, and re-earn every skill with pass rates pinned to the exact model that produced them.

$ skillmaker run

You inherited skills you didn't write.

A teammate left, or you're vendoring a public repo. Reading a SKILL.md tells you what it says — not whether it works. Adopt wraps every file as a bundle without moving it (QA'd on real public repos: 59/60 and 39/39), and graded runs turn “seems fine” into n · pass rate · confidence interval.

$ skillmaker adopt

Your team is standardizing on skills.

Five people, five private variants of the same skill, and you've been asked to publish the blessed set. Skills clear a review gate before publishing to a marketplace manifest, each carrying its design rationale and measurements — so “why do we trust this one” has an answer that isn't you.

$ skillmaker publish
01

Design is a first-class artifact.

Every skill carries a design doc: intent, triggers, the workflow, failure hypotheses. Six months later you'll know why the skill is shaped the way it is — and so will the next person.

02

Evals with honest math.

Fixture cases by failure class — golden, refusal, empty-input, rerun, hard-case — mapped against five risk families. Coverage (“a fixture exists”) and validation (“passes at 28/30 on claude-code”) never merge. A single run is a sample, not a measurement.

03

A production line, not a folder.

Skills move idea → researching → drafting → evaluating → published through guarded gates: forward requires an approved review, publishing requires the gate, and moving backward is legal — regression is a modeled fact. Every decision lands on an append-only, git-tracked journal.

How it works

  1. Step 1 skillmaker new

    Scaffold a Skill Bundle.

    skillmaker new <slug> creates a design doc, fixture slot, output slot, and stations for the skill — a real bundle, not a bare file.

  2. Step 2

    Build and prove.

    Draft SKILL.md from the design, add fixtures by failure class, run them against real agents, grade the read-outs.

  3. Step 3 skillmaker advance

    Move through the gates.

    Advance the bundle through idea → researching → drafting → evaluating → published, with the journal recording every judgment. Ship the output; keep the asset.

skillmaker in 30 seconds 
$ skillmaker init
skillmaker: initialized workspace at ~/projects/my-skills

$ skillmaker new bash-guardrails --name "Bash Guardrails"
skillmaker: created bundle bash-guardrails

$ skillmaker status bash-guardrails
slug:        bash-guardrails
name:        Bash Guardrails
stage:       idea
substate:    working
events:      1
last event:  bundle.created at 2026-07-11T09:47:31.618Z
fixtures:    0
coverage:    0 covered, 0 partial, 0 gap

$ skillmaker start
skillmaker: serving my-skills at http://localhost:4322

vs. the eval bench

The score is the easy part.

An eval platform tells you the score. It can't tell you what to test, which version earned the number, why it drifted, or how to fix it. That's the other 90% of the job.

The question you're actually asking Eval bench Skillmaker
What should I even test? You figure it out (in TypeScript) Agents co-design evals with you, in plain English
Is the tested thing the shipped thing? You maintain a synced copy The skill itself is what runs — nothing to sync
Which version earned this number? Experiment names, if you're disciplined Pinned automatically: skill version + model
It drifted — why? Here's the score delta Here's the trail: what changed, and the research behind each choice
Now fix it Back to your editor The operating table is the same surface

Instrumenting an API product with an eng team? Braintrust is genuinely good at that. But if you're on the hook for skills that ride inside agents, a bench in another tab is the schlep, not the solution.

What honest numbers look like

We don't have customer logos to show you — pre-alpha, no customers yet. What we have is our own QA logs: a hand-written skill adopted from a real repo, measured, revised, and measured again on claude-code.

version n pass rate 95% ci label
as adopted 3 67% [21%, 94%] below smoke
v2 (revised) 3 100% [44%, 100%] below smoke

Both cells say “below smoke” because three runs is a sample, not a proof — the product refuses to let you over-trust the numbers, even when they flatter us. That refusal is the product.

If your skills are load-bearing and you want receipts on them, we're working hands-on with a first round of design partners — open an issue and tell us what broke.

We can't make your agent predictable — nobody can; that's what you hired it for. We can make sure nothing drifts silently, nothing fails anonymously, and nothing stays wrong. Free, local, phones nothing home. One command on the repo you already have.

The only thing you risk is finding out.

More under the hood

Two doors, one ground.

CLI and live board share the same journal — agents work the CLI, humans judge in the browser, nothing desyncs.

Version-pinned evidence.

Measurements bind to the content hash they exercised. Change the skill, and the numbers honestly reset to “not yet measured.”

Drift you can see.

Hand-finish your SKILL.md freely — the studio shows that it diverged from the design, and when, without policing it.

Journal-native todos.

The work-tracking lives in the same event stream as everything else. git log is your audit trail.

Graded read-outs.

Grade a run pass, fail, or partial — in the CLI or the board — and watch it join the read-out as n · pass-rate · confidence interval, per fixture, version, and provider. Never pooled.

Desktop app.

A double-clickable macOS shell around the same board and journal — no terminal required. Build it from source today; a signed download is on the roadmap.

The skillbook.

Generated docs for your whole skill set — design rationale, changelogs, and version-pinned pass rates inline, one page per skill.

Agent-first production.

Stations run over ACP: an agent researches, drafts, and evaluates; you approve at the gates. William's own skills ship through this loop.

Publish with receipts.

Send a skill's output to a git directory or a marketplace manifest once it clears the gate — the same guarded path from the CLI or the board.

Adopt an existing repo.

Already have a folder of SKILL.md files? Wrap them as bundles in place — no files moved, no rewrite required.

Two providers, one measurement.

claude-code and codex are both full eval peers — the same fixture, measured per provider, never pooled together.

Questions

I can just write a SKILL.md by hand.
You can — and Skillmaker won't stop you (hand-edits are legal, drift is displayed, not punished). The studio earns its keep the first time a skill misbehaves and you need to know what changed, what was tested, and what was approved.
Is this only for Claude?
Bundles target any ACP-drivable agent. claude-code and codex are both full eval peers today — one skill, measured per provider, never pooled together. Publishing to a Codex-format marketplace manifest is still best-effort (no published spec to conform to yet); the git-directory and Claude-marketplace targets are on solid ground.
We already have a repo full of skills. Do we have to restructure it?
No. skillmaker adopt wraps every existing SKILL.md as a bundle in place — no files move, your layout stays yours. It's been QA'd against real public skills repos with very different structures (59/60 and 39/39 adopted). Evidence accrues from there: add fixtures to the skills you actually depend on first.
Where does my data live?
In your repo. Files plus a git-tracked JSONL journal. SQLite is a throwaway index. No server, no account.
